title: Family and childhood information
description: Born in Viola, parents moved to Idaho from Scotland in 1888, had 9 children including Fannie, bought a homestead from the Sanders family; she attended school in Viola and Moscow.

title: Father's background
description: Her parents were David and Mary Cuthbert; her father logged and harvested, cleared land.

title: 1893 wet harvest was good for her father
description: The 1893 wet harvest that devastated so many families was actually good for her family; he raised cattle that could graze all over, feed was cheap.

title: Parents from Scotland
description: Her parents moved from Scotland to the United States three times, lived in Iowa, Idaho.

title: Fire in Viola
description: Fire set by teenage boys burnt down the church and schoolhouse; boys set other things on fire purposely.

title: Strawberry patches
description: Her family grew many strawberry patches, both for food and used to trade for goods in the summer months, saved up to trade things when prosperous.

title: Hotel in Viola
description: Buggies with tops were a big deal; Viola had a three-story hotel - her mother used to keep a few rooms there in the winter months during the school week, so much snow they could not travel back home during the week. Housekeeping rooms allwoed them to prepare food and not eat out a lot.

title: Doctors in Viola
description: There were two doctors in Viola, Dr. Barnum, and another doctor known for helping a man who cut off his legs accidentally in a feed grinder.

title: Sawmill in Viola
description: Sawmill was nearby Viola, employed most of the town. Boarding school up on the hill, later moved near main road.

title: Businesses in Viola
description: Pool hall was really a shack, livery stable, some gambling. Sawmill kept people employed in spring and fall, people played cards in between.

title: Women's life in Viola
description: Women would congregate at someone’s home, parties, playing cards. Stories about Harrison family. Talk about the old days.

title: People not caring about what other thought
description: People didn't keep up with the Jones's as much as they do now; people didn't accumulate so much stuff.

title: Fannie was a cook
description: Fannie received $5.00 per day as a cook (not good money), she traveled around with threshing crews and took care of horses. When feed got too expensive, tractors were being introduced.

title: How she met her husband
description: Met her husband because his family farmed on other side of Moscow. Train went to town then. Story of man being struck by lightning. Her husband worked in the woods and as a farmer.

title: Stories about her sister; parents lived to old age
description: Her sister was trained as a nurse locally who moved to Coos Bay, Oregon. Her sister met her husband there before she traveled to France during World War I. Didn’t return to this area until 1940, moved to Walla Walla after husband died. Father lived to age 99, mother to age 92.

title: Stories of neighborhood kids
description: Stories of neighborhoods kids, used to have a raft to get over the creek in spring. Two small girls died near the creek when she was a child.
